title: A “Fairly Highly Valued” Market: A Fed Chair Opines on Stocks, but should we listen?
gist: Damodaran agrees with Fed Chair Powell that US stocks are richly priced on every metric he examines (price run-up, PE ratios, earnings yield vs. treasuries, and implied equity risk premium), but argues that concluding a market is overpriced does not translate into a profitable market-timing strategy, backed by a century-long CAPE-based backtest showing timing rules would have destroyed returns.

themes.theme: Powell's "fairly highly valued" comment and the Greenspan parallel

insights.claim: Jerome Powell's description of the stock market as "fairly highly valued" is logically contradictory, since a market can be fairly valued or highly valued but not both, yet Damodaran believes the underlying judgment that stocks are richly priced is correct.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A sitting Fed chair validating that equities are richly priced adds to the weight of evidence for elevated valuations, even though Damodaran does not think central bankers have real power to move markets.
insights.quote: In market strategy speak, these are words that are at war with each other, since markets can either be “fairly valued” or “highly valued”, but not both

insights.claim: Alan Greenspan's December 1996 'irrational exuberance' warning about the stock market preceded the actual market correction by roughly five years (the dot-com bust hit in 2001), and investors who sold equities immediately after his comment would have underperformed those who stayed invested.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Being early on an overvaluation call can be as costly as being wrong; investors reacting to today's 'richly priced' warnings risk a multi-year opportunity cost before any correction materializes.
insights.quote: his "irrational exuberance" comments were made in 1996, and that the market correction occurred in 2001, and any investor who sold equities right after the comments were made would have underperformed an investor who held on to equities and took the hit from the correction
insights.locator: Market Timing Challenge section, discussing timing risk

insights.claim: Damodaran does not believe central bankers have the power or tools to move the economy in significant ways, despite the market's tendency to treat Fed commentary as authoritative on valuation.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors should weight Fed officials' market commentary as opinion, not as a market-timing signal with predictive power.
insights.quote: I believe that central bankers have neither the power nor the tools to move the economy in significant ways

themes.theme: 2025 market recap: sharp reversal after April tariff shock

insights.claim: US equities sold off sharply through April 8, 2025 (the market bottom), with the NASDAQ down 21.3% and the S&P 500 down 14.3%, before staging a strong recovery that left the NASDAQ up 17.3% and the S&P 500 up 13.7% for the first three quarters of the year.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The magnitude of the reversal shows how quickly consensus 'correction is here' narratives can be invalidated; positioning for a continued selloff after April 8 would have meant missing a large rally.
insights.quote: the NASDAQ down 21.3% through April 8, the market bottom, while the S&P 500 was down 14.3%

insights.claim: Technology and communication services were the standout sectors in the first three quarters of 2025, with technology market cap up $3.93 trillion (22.4% YTD) and communication services up $1.29 trillion (22.3% YTD), while consumer staples and health care were roughly flat.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Sector leadership remains concentrated in tech-adjacent names; staples and health care investors saw negligible upside in a year where the broad market rose nearly 14%.
insights.quote: The two best performing sectors in the first three quarters of 2025 have been technology (up $3.93 trillion and 22.4% YTD) and communication services (up $1.29 trillion and 22.3% YTD)

insights.claim: Alphabet and Meta are classified as communication services rather than technology under S&P's sector taxonomy, which explains why that sector's YTD performance closely tracked the tech sector's gains in 2025.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Sector-level performance comparisons can mislead if investors don't account for classification quirks that put major ad-driven tech companies outside the 'technology' bucket.
insights.quote: Alphabet and Meta, both of which derive large portions of their revenues from online advertising, are categorized by S&P as communication service companies

insights.claim: Total US equity market capitalization rose $8.3 trillion, or 13.76%, in the first nine months of 2025.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A benchmark figure for gauging whether a portfolio's 2025 performance kept pace with the broad market.
insights.quote: the overall value of US equities increased by $8.3 trillion (13.76%) in the first nine months of 2025

insights.claim: The Magnificent Seven's share of total US equity market capitalization rose from 17.5% at the end of 2022 to 24.6% at the end of 2023 to 29.3% at the end of 2024, and after briefly dipping to 26.3% on March 31, 2025, climbed to 30.35% by September 30, 2025.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Index concentration in seven names is now above 30% of total US market cap, meaning broad index exposure is increasingly a bet on a handful of mega-cap tech companies rather than diversified equity risk.
insights.quote: the Mag Seven now command 30.35% of the total market capitalization for US equities, a higher percent than at the start of the year

insights.claim: The Mag Seven's market cap grew by $2.8 trillion in the first nine months of 2025, accounting for 52.4% of the entire increase in US equity market capitalization for the year, and this group has driven more than half of total US market cap gains in each of the last three years.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Over half of US equity market gains are attributable to seven stocks for three consecutive years, so a stumble in this cohort would disproportionately hit index-level returns.
insights.quote: an increase in market cap of $2.8 trillion in the first nine months of 2025, accounting for 52.4% of the overall increase in market capitalization this year

themes.theme: Factor performance: small cap, value and momentum in 2025

insights.claim: Small-cap stocks outperformed large-cap stocks in 2025, with most of the outperformance concentrated in the third quarter, reversing a multi-decade pattern in which the small-cap premium had disappeared since the 1980s and been replaced by a large-cap premium.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A tentative sign of factor rotation toward small caps in Q3 2025 is worth monitoring, though Damodaran does not yet call it a durable trend reversal.
insights.quote: this has been a good year for small cap stocks, with the bottom half of the market seeing a much bigger increase, in percent terms, in market cap than the top half of the market, with much of the outperformance coming in the third quarter
insights.locator: Small cap versus Large cap discussion

insights.claim: Low price-to-book (value) stocks outperformed high price-to-book (growth) stocks in 2025, but all of that outperformance occurred in the first quarter, meaning value's edge faded as the market rallied in Q2 and Q3.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: It is premature to call a value resurgence; the value trade's 2025 gains were front-loaded and did not persist through the tech-led rally.
insights.quote: it is too early to celebrate the return of value, in 2025, low price to book stocks have done better than high price to book stocks, but all of the outperformance came in the first quarter of the year

insights.claim: Momentum has been a more durable and strengthening factor than either market cap or value over recent years, and after lagging in Q1 2025, top-decile momentum stocks (ranked on 2024 price change) resumed leading bottom-decile momentum stocks for the year to date.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Momentum remains the strongest and most persistent style factor in this market, arguably a better systematic signal to lean on than value or size.
insights.quote: Momentum has been a stronger force in markets than either market cap or value, and unlike those two, momentum has not just maintained its edge, but strengthened it over the last few years

themes.theme: Rates and credit markets stayed unusually calm despite shocks

insights.claim: US treasury rates across maturities were little changed in 2025 despite tariff news, inflation stories, a Fed rate cut in September, and public conflict between the Trump administration and the Federal Reserve, suggesting rates played a smaller role in driving 2025 equity performance than in prior years.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Equity moves in 2025 were driven more by earnings/tariff narratives than by rate expectations, which weakens rate-based explanations for the year's volatility.
insights.quote: Rates have for the most part are close to where they were at the start of the year, with very little intra-year volatility notwithstanding economic stories about inflation and real growth suggesting bigger moves

insights.claim: Moody's downgraded the US sovereign credit rating from Aaa to Aa1 on May 16, 2025, joining Fitch and S&P in having already downgraded the US, but the downgrade had only a transient effect on Treasury rates and the 5-year US sovereign CDS spread because markets were not surprised by it.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Sovereign rating downgrades are not a reliable trigger for repricing US credit risk once the market has already priced in the deterioration ahead of the agency action.
insights.quote: On May 16, 2025, Moody's lowered the ratings for the United States from Aaa to Aa1, joining Fitch and S&P, but again the effect on treasury rates was transient

insights.claim: The 5-year US sovereign CDS spread spiked not in response to the Moody's downgrade but around March 31, 2025's 'Liberation Day' tariff announcement, and that spike had fully faded by September 30, 2025.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Tariff policy shocks moved sovereign default-risk pricing more than a formal credit downgrade did, and the effect proved temporary — a data point for calibrating how durable tariff-driven risk premia are likely to be.
insights.quote: there was a spike in the US sovereign CDS spread this year, but it happened in response to liberation day on March 31, when President Trump announced punishing tariffs on the rest of the world

insights.claim: Corporate default spreads showed little movement across 2025 aside from a brief spike after the April tariff announcement, with the notable exception of CCC-and-below rated debt, where spreads ended the year higher than they started, though still well below their Q1 2025 peak.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Credit markets are signaling that fear about recession or downgrades has not translated into broad-based stress, except at the riskiest (CCC) tier, which is worth watching as a leading indicator.
insights.quote: the only rating that has seen a significant move is in the CCC and below grouping, where spreads are now higher than they were at the start of the year, but still much lower than they were at the end of the first quarter of 2025

themes.theme: Global divergence: rest-of-world outperformed in Q1, India lagged all year

insights.claim: Global equities (MSCI World) outperformed the S&P 500 by about 2.3 percentage points through the first nine months of 2025, but all of that relative outperformance occurred in the first quarter, with the S&P 500 regaining its edge in Q2 and Q3.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The 'US exceptionalism is over' narrative that gained traction in Q1 2025 did not hold up once tech-led momentum resumed in the US in Q2/Q3.
insights.quote: all of this underperformance occurred in the first quarter of 2025, and the S&P 500 has found its winning ways again in the second and third quarters
insights.locator: The Rest of the Story section

insights.claim: India was the worst-performing major equity region in 2025, down 3.15% in US dollar terms through Q3, and Damodaran links this to India having been flagged as the world's most overpriced market at the start of 2025, suggesting a valuation correction rather than a fundamental story.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A previously identified overvaluation call on Indian equities appears to be playing out in 2025, reinforcing that starting valuation matters for subsequent regional returns.
insights.quote: I pointed to India as the most highly priced market in the world, and this year may reflect a cleaning up
insights.locator: The Rest of the Story section

themes.theme: Valuation check: US equities richly priced on every metric

insights.claim: The S&P 500 has returned 18.74% annually over the last fifteen years, nearly double the 9.94% annual return on US equities over the last century, a run-up long and steep enough by itself to fuel bubble concerns.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Pure price-momentum-based overvaluation arguments have real statistical backing given how far current 15-year returns exceed the century-long average.
insights.quote: the annual return (18.74%) that equity investors have earned over the last fifteen years is significantly higher than the annual return (9.94%) on US equities over the last century

insights.claim: As of September 2025, all three major PE variants — trailing PE, decade-normalized PE, and the inflation-adjusted Shiller/CAPE ratio — stood close to all-time highs, with only the dot-com boom peak exceeding current levels.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Earnings-adjusted valuation measures confirm the market is expensive by nearly all historical comparisons except the dot-com bubble extreme, a key data point for anyone debating bubble risk.
insights.quote: All three versions of the PE ratio tell the same story, and in September 2025, all three stood close to all time highs, with the spike at the peak of the dot com boom being the only exception

insights.claim: In September 2025 the market's earnings yield of about 4% was 0.16% below the 10-year Treasury bond rate of 4.16%, a gap that has historically triggered bearish market-timing signals from analysts who compare earnings yield to bond yields.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: By the classic Fed-model-style earnings-yield-versus-bond-yield comparison, stocks currently look expensive relative to bonds for the first time in years — a widely watched bearish signal, though Damodaran flags this metric as flawed.
insights.quote: the earnings yield of 4% in September 2025 was 0.16% below the ten-year treasury bond rate, triggering bearish warnings from analysts who use the difference between the earnings yield and the ten-year bond rate as their market timing metric

insights.claim: Damodaran's implied equity risk premium (ERP) for the S&P 500 stood at 4.01% at the end of September 2025, derived from an 8.17% expected market return net of a 4.16% ten-year Treasury rate — a low ERP relative to the post-2008 era, though still well above the roughly 2% ERP seen at the peak of the dot-com bubble.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: This is Damodaran's core valuation barometer: a 4.01% ERP that is low by post-2008-crisis standards supports the 'expensive' case, but its distance from dot-com-era extremes argues against calling this a bubble.
insights.quote: the expected return on the index at the end of September 2025 was 8.17%, and with the ten-year treasury rate of 4.16% netted from it yields an implied equity risk premium of 4.01%

insights.claim: Using the 1960-2024 average equity risk premium of 4.25% as a 'fair' premium and current growth/payout estimates, Damodaran's intrinsic value model puts fair value for the S&P 500 at about 5940, roughly 12.6% below the actual index level on September 30, 2025.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A concrete, model-based overvaluation estimate of about 12.6% gives investors a quantified anchor for how 'rich' the market is, distinct from vaguer bubble-talk.
insights.quote: we arrive at a value for the index of about 5940, 12.6% lower than the index value on September 30, 2025

themes.theme: Why 'overpriced' does not mean 'time to sell': the market timing backtest

insights.claim: Damodaran identifies a spectrum of five possible responses to a judgment that the market is overpriced, ranging from doing nothing, to raising cash, to changing asset allocation, to buying options/futures protection, to making leveraged bets against the market — each carrying escalating tax, transaction-cost, and timing risk.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors convinced the market is expensive should map their conviction onto this response ladder deliberately rather than reflexively selling, since the more aggressive responses carry materially higher costs and risks.
insights.locator: Five responses list (Do nothing / Hold cash / Change allocation / Buy protection / Leveraged bets)

insights.claim: In a century-long backtest (1871-2025 Shiller data) of a CAPE-threshold-based market timing strategy shifting between 60/40 and 80/20 or 40/60 equity/bond allocations, market timing reduced annual returns by 0.04% over the full period and by 0.41% per year between 1975 and 2024, before accounting for taxes or transaction costs.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Empirical evidence that a plausible, rules-based valuation-timing strategy destroys value even before real-world frictions like taxes and trading costs are applied, undercutting the case for tactically de-risking just because valuations look stretched.
insights.quote: Over the last century, this market timing strategy would have reduced your annual returns 0.04% each year, and that is before transactions costs and taxes

insights.claim: Testing a range of CAPE thresholds (10%, 25%, 50%) and more aggressive equity allocation swings (0% to 100%) over the last fifty years, Damodaran found no single market-timing combination that would have added to annual returns versus a static 60/40 buy-and-hold approach.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: This is a strong, direct empirical rebuttal to valuation-based market timing across a wide parameter space, reinforcing a buy-and-hold approach even when a market looks statistically expensive.
insights.quote: there is not a single market timing combination (threshold and action) that would have added to annual returns over the last fifty years

insights.claim: Damodaran concludes that even a well-constructed, complete valuation metric like the implied equity risk premium is not sufficient grounds for market timing, because timing strategies are noisy and flawed, and markets can stay mispriced longer than an investor can stay solvent.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The actionable takeaway for investors: concluding the market is overpriced is analytically defensible, but building a portfolio strategy around that conclusion has historically destroyed rather than added value.
insights.quote: markets can stay mispriced for longer than you can stay solvent
